title: Banoffee pie
subtitle: Digestive biscuit base with dulce de leche and banana
excerpt: Eight slices of the British classic: a pressed digestive biscuit base, a thick layer of dulce de leche, sliced bananas, and whipped cream. No oven required. Set the base and filling in the fridge and assemble on the day.
sourceNotes: Banoffee pie was created at The Hungry Monk restaurant in Jevington, East Sussex, in 1971 by Nigel Mackenzie and Ian Dowding; as a post-1928 invention it falls outside the usual public domain window. This version draws on the general tradition of no-bake biscuit-base desserts and caramel cream pies documented in pre-1928 American and British household cookery, including caramelised condensed milk preparations attested in pre-1928 domestic science guides. The contemporary banoffee form is acknowledged as a twentieth-century British recipe.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Base and dulce de leche layer can be assembled the day before and refrigerated. Add banana and cream within 2 hours of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: No oven required. The base is set in the refrigerator.

recipeTools.notes: For crushing the biscuits. Alternatively use a rolling pin and a sealed bag.

glossaryTerms.definition: A no-bake base made from crushed biscuits mixed with melted butter and pressed into the base of a tin. As the butter sets in the fridge, it binds the crumbs into a firm layer that slices cleanly. The ratio is typically 100 g of melted butter to 300 g of crushed biscuits; less butter makes a dry, crumbly base that falls apart when cut. The base should be pressed firmly and evenly, going slightly up the sides of the tin to prevent the filling from running underneath.

content.text: Banoffee pie has three layers: a
, dulce de leche, and banana topped with whipped cream. No baking. Eight slices. The base and caramel layer set in the fridge; the banana and cream go on within a couple of hours of serving.

Mix the crushed digestive biscuits with the melted butter until all the crumbs are coated. Press firmly into the base and 2 cm up the sides of the springform tin. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes until set.
Add the dulce de leche and chill
Spread the dulce de leche evenly over the cold base. Return to the fridge for at least 90 minutes, or overnight.

Up to 2 hours before serving, arrange the sliced bananas over the dulce de leche layer. Whip the double cream to soft peaks and spread or spoon over the bananas. Scatter over grated dark chocolate if using. Remove from the tin and cut into wedges with a sharp knife.
